title: Origami windmill
subtitle: Folding a classic pinwheel windmill from a single square
excerpt: The origami windmill is a flat model with four angled blades radiating from a central hub. It is one of the simplest models that uses the preliminary square base, and the final form demonstrates how a sequence of fold-and-squash steps produces rotational symmetry from a square sheet. Mounted on a pencil it rotates in the wind.
sourceNotes: The origami windmill (kaza-guruma) is a traditional Japanese fold documented across public-domain origami teaching materials. The construction described here follows the standard squash-fold method.

glossaryTerms.definition: An origami move where a point or flap is opened and pressed flat symmetrically. The paper is literally squashed open into a new shape. Used in the windmill to create the four blade shapes from triangular flaps.

A standard starting form in origami made by folding the square in half both ways and along both diagonals, then collapsing into a smaller square. The starting point for many traditional models including the windmill, the bird base, and the crane.

content.text: The windmill model uses both valley and mountain folds as well as the
. For a first attempt, use a duo paper (coloured both sides, different colours each side) so the blade faces and the central square are easily distinguished while learning the sequence.

Fold the square in half horizontally, crease and unfold. Fold in half vertically, crease and unfold. Fold along both diagonals, crease and unfold. The paper now has a crease map: four valley folds dividing it into eight triangular sections. This crease map makes the subsequent collapse steps much easier.

Hold the square with one corner pointing toward you. Push the left and right corners toward each other: the horizontal fold becomes a mountain fold and the model collapses into a smaller square with four flaps, two at the front and two at the back. This is the preliminary base. The open end (where the flaps are) points upward.

Working with two front flaps: fold each front flap's outer edge to the vertical centre line, crease and unfold. The two crease lines form a V. Open one flap and squash it flat along the crease line into a small diamond shape. Repeat for the second flap. Turn the model over and repeat for the two back flaps. The model now shows four diamond shapes arranged around a central square.

Rotate the model 45 degrees so the blades point diagonally. Press the central square flat and reinforce all eight blade edges with the bone folder for a crisp finished model. To mount on a pencil: push the pencil eraser through the centre of the model and the windmill will spin in a light breeze. The 20 cm kami version spins more freely than the 15 cm; heavier papers spin less easily.
